description: Diagram showing triplet loss with an anchor sample, one positive sample (same class, pulled closer), and one negative sample (different class, pushed apart), with margin epsilon illustrated as the minimum required distance gap.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the geometric intuition of triplet loss — students can see how the loss enforces a margin between positive and negative distances in embedding space, making the abstract math concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing triplet loss, immediately after presenting the loss formula, to help students map the mathematical terms (anchor, x+, x-, epsilon) to a spatial diagram.
suggested_caption: Triplet loss: anchor pulls positives close, pushes negatives apart. (Schroff et al., 2015)

description: Side-by-side comparison of three loss functions — contrastive loss, triplet loss, and lifted structured loss — using graph diagrams where red edges connect similar pairs and blue edges connect dissimilar pairs, showing how each method uses increasingly more pairwise information per training step.
teaching_value: Allows students to directly compare the scope of pairwise relationships exploited by each loss, making it clear why lifted structured loss is more data-efficient by leveraging all pairs in a batch rather than just one or three samples.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from triplet loss to lifted structured loss, to motivate why using all pairs in a batch is beneficial over single-pair or single-triplet approaches.
suggested_caption: Contrastive, triplet, and lifted structured loss compared by pair usage. (Song et al., 2015)

description: Visualization illustrating sampling bias (false negatives) in contrastive learning, showing how samples that are semantically similar to the anchor can be incorrectly treated as negatives, and the resulting performance degradation.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the false negative problem in unsupervised contrastive learning, helping students understand why naive random negative sampling is flawed and motivating debiased contrastive objectives.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the problem of false negatives before presenting the debiased contrastive loss formulation, to build intuition for why correction is needed.
suggested_caption: False negative sampling bias degrades contrastive learning performance. (Chuang et al., 2020)

description: t-SNE 2D visualization comparing learned representations with and without debiased contrastive learning, showing that debiased training produces better-separated, more coherent clusters per class.
teaching_value: Provides empirical visual evidence that correcting for sampling bias improves representation quality, helping students connect the theoretical motivation for debiasing to measurable outcomes in embedding space.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the debiased contrastive loss formula, as evidence that the correction leads to qualitatively better learned representations.
suggested_caption: Debiased contrastive learning yields better-separated t-SNE clusters. (Chuang et al., 2020)

description: Pseudo-code block showing implementations of three objectives side by side: standard NCE loss, debiased contrastive loss, and hard negative sampling objective, with M=1, written in Python-style code.
teaching_value: Bridges theory and practice by showing exactly how each loss variant is computed algorithmically, enabling students to understand the incremental modifications from NCE to debiased to hard-negative-weighted objectives.
when_to_show: Show after explaining all three loss variants mathematically, as a synthesis step to help students see how the equations translate into code and how the methods relate to each other.
suggested_caption: Pseudo-code comparing NCE, debiased, and hard-negative contrastive losses. (Robinson et al., 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of SimCLR showing: an input image augmented into two views, each passed through an encoder f(·) and projection head g(·), with the NT-Xent contrastive loss applied to the projected embeddings to maximize agreement between the two views.
teaching_value: Clearly shows the full SimCLR pipeline — from data augmentation to encoder to projection head to loss — helping students understand how self-supervised contrastive learning is implemented end-to-end without labels.
when_to_show: Show when introducing SimCLR as the canonical example of augmentation-based contrastive learning, to give students a complete architectural overview before diving into loss details.
suggested_caption: SimCLR: two augmented views trained to share embeddings via contrastive loss. (Chen et al., 2020)

description: Formal algorithm pseudocode for SimCLR detailing the minibatch sampling, augmentation, forward pass through encoder and projection head, pairwise similarity computation, and NT-Xent loss calculation steps.
teaching_value: Provides a precise, step-by-step algorithmic description of SimCLR training, allowing students to understand exactly how positive and negative pairs are constructed from a batch and how the loss is computed.
when_to_show: Show after the SimCLR architecture diagram, when students need to understand the training procedure in detail or are preparing to implement it.
suggested_caption: SimCLR training algorithm: augment, encode, project, compute NT-Xent loss. (Chen et al., 2020)

description: Pipeline diagram of Barlow Twins showing two distorted image views fed through twin networks, producing embeddings whose cross-correlation matrix is computed and pushed toward the identity matrix via the Barlow Twins loss.
teaching_value: Illustrates the key conceptual innovation of Barlow Twins — using cross-correlation rather than direct similarity — helping students understand how it avoids representational collapse without negative pairs.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Barlow Twins as an alternative to SimCLR, to highlight the architectural similarity but conceptual difference in the objective function.
suggested_caption: Barlow Twins pushes cross-correlation matrix of twin outputs toward identity. (Zbontar et al., 2021)

description: PyTorch-style pseudo-code for the Barlow Twins algorithm, showing network forward passes, cross-correlation matrix computation along the batch dimension, and the invariance plus redundancy-reduction loss terms.
teaching_value: Translates the Barlow Twins mathematical objective into concrete code, helping students understand how the cross-correlation matrix is computed in practice and how the two loss terms (diagonal vs. off-diagonal) are implemented.
when_to_show: Show after the Barlow Twins architecture diagram and loss formula, for students who want to understand implementation details or compare with SimCLR's algorithm.
suggested_caption: Barlow Twins PyTorch pseudo-code: cross-correlation toward identity matrix. (Zbontar et al., 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of BYOL showing an online network (encoder, projector, predictor) and a target network (encoder, projector only) with stop-gradient on the target, polyak-averaged weight updates, and the MSE loss between online predictor output and target projector output.
teaching_value: Illustrates how BYOL achieves self-supervised learning without any negative samples by using an asymmetric architecture with a momentum-updated target network, helping students understand why the stop-gradient and EMA are critical to prevent collapse.
when_to_show: Show when contrasting BYOL with SimCLR to highlight that negative pairs are not strictly necessary, and to explain the role of the asymmetric predictor and EMA target in preventing representational collapse.
suggested_caption: BYOL: online network learns from momentum target network without negative pairs. (Grill et al., 2020)

description: Training pipeline diagram for instance-level contrastive learning showing how image embeddings are L2-normalized and compared against a memory bank of stored representations to compute a non-parametric softmax loss.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize how a memory bank stores past embeddings to avoid recomputing all negatives each batch, illustrating the compute-vs-staleness tradeoff central to early contrastive learning.
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining why computing all negative samples per batch is expensive and how a memory bank addresses this; use before introducing MoCo as the motivating predecessor.
suggested_caption: Instance discrimination pipeline with memory bank for negative samples. (Wu et al., 2018)

description: Diagram of MoCo (Momentum Contrast) showing a query encoder and a momentum-updated key encoder feeding into a queue-based dictionary, with arrows indicating how query and key representations are compared via InfoNCE loss.
teaching_value: Visually contrasts MoCo's queue-based dictionary with the memory bank approach, making the momentum encoder update rule and the FIFO queue mechanism concrete and easy to compare.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the memory bank limitation; use to explain how MoCo's queue enables reuse of recent mini-batch representations and why momentum updates stabilize training.
suggested_caption: MoCo uses a momentum encoder and queue dictionary for contrastive learning. (He et al., 2019)

description: Architecture diagram of CURL showing two augmented views of a reinforcement learning observation being passed through a query encoder and a momentum key encoder, with contrastive loss computed between their embeddings.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how contrastive learning principles (MoCo-style momentum encoder, data augmentation) transfer from vision to reinforcement learning, helping students generalize the framework beyond image classification.
when_to_show: Use when discussing applications of contrastive learning beyond standard vision benchmarks, or when illustrating how MoCo-V2 ideas generalize to RL observation spaces.
suggested_caption: CURL applies momentum-contrast contrastive learning to RL observations. (Srinivas et al., 2020)

description: Flowchart of the DeepCluster iterative pipeline: a CNN extracts features, k-means clustering assigns pseudo-labels, and those labels supervise the next round of CNN training in a loop.
teaching_value: Illustrates the iterative self-supervised clustering paradigm as an alternative to pairwise contrastive loss, helping students understand pseudo-label generation and the risk of trivial solutions.
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from pairwise contrastive methods to clustering-based self-supervised methods; use to motivate why SwAV was designed to avoid DeepCluster's costly offline clustering phase.
suggested_caption: DeepCluster iteratively clusters features and uses assignments as pseudo-labels. (Caron et al., 2018)

description: Side-by-side comparison diagram of SwAV versus standard contrastive instance learning, showing how SwAV replaces direct pairwise comparison with prototype/code assignment and a swapped prediction loss between two augmented views.
teaching_value: Clarifies the key architectural innovation of SwAV — swapping codes between views rather than directly comparing embeddings — making the prototype-based approach visually distinct from standard contrastive methods.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how SwAV avoids explicit negative pairs while still preventing collapse; use the side-by-side layout to highlight what changes relative to instance contrastive learning.
suggested_caption: SwAV swaps cluster codes between views instead of direct contrastive comparison. (Caron et al., 2020)

description: Diagram of CLIP's contrastive pre-training showing an N×N similarity matrix between N image embeddings and N text embeddings, with diagonal entries (correct pairs) maximized and off-diagonal entries (incorrect pairs) minimized.
teaching_value: Makes the symmetric cross-entropy contrastive objective over image-text pairs concrete; the N×N matrix visualization directly shows why N correct pairs and N(N-1) incorrect pairs arise from a single batch.
when_to_show: Use when introducing multimodal contrastive learning; the matrix diagram is ideal for explaining the batch-level pairing objective before discussing zero-shot transfer results.
suggested_caption: CLIP maximizes similarity for N correct image-text pairs in an N×N matrix. (Radford et al., 2021)

description: NumPy-style pseudocode for the CLIP training algorithm, showing image and text encoder forward passes, L2 normalization, cosine similarity matrix computation, and symmetric cross-entropy loss calculation.
teaching_value: Translates the mathematical CLIP objective into executable pseudocode, allowing students to map each equation component (encoders, normalization, similarity, loss) to concrete implementation steps.
when_to_show: Show after the CLIP architecture diagram when students need to understand the exact training procedure; particularly useful for students with programming backgrounds who benefit from code-level clarity.
suggested_caption: NumPy pseudocode for CLIP's symmetric contrastive training objective. (Radford et al., 2021)

description: Line graph comparing data efficiency (ImageNet accuracy vs. number of training examples seen) across different pre-training objectives and text encoding strategies, showing that bag-of-words + contrastive objective achieves the best efficiency.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for why CLIP's design choices (BoW encoding + contrastive loss vs. generative caption prediction) matter, teaching students to evaluate architectural decisions through data efficiency curves.
when_to_show: Use when discussing CLIP's training objective design choices; show after the architecture diagram to justify why contrastive loss outperforms predictive captioning in terms of sample efficiency.
suggested_caption: Contrastive + bag-of-words encoding yields large data efficiency gains for CLIP. (Radford et al., 2021)

description: Comparison diagram of self-supervised versus supervised contrastive loss formulations, showing that supervised contrastive learning treats all same-class samples as positives (not just augmented pairs), while self-supervised only uses augmented views of the same image.
teaching_value: Clearly distinguishes the positive-pair definition between supervised and self-supervised contrastive learning, helping students understand how label information changes the loss landscape and can improve representation quality.
when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from self-supervised to supervised contrastive methods; use the visual contrast to prompt discussion of how leveraging labels changes what counts as a 'positive' example.
suggested_caption: Supervised contrastive learning uses all same-class samples as positives. (Khosla et al., 2021)

description: Schematic diagram showing three text augmentation strategies: token cutoff (randomly masking individual tokens), feature cutoff (masking entire feature dimensions), and span cutoff (masking contiguous spans of tokens) applied to a sentence.
teaching_value: Visually distinguishes three distinct cutoff augmentation strategies for text contrastive learning, helping students understand how different masking granularities create augmented views of the same sentence.
when_to_show: When introducing text-specific augmentation techniques for contrastive learning, especially to contrast with image augmentation methods and show how NLP adapts the concept.
suggested_caption: Three cutoff augmentation strategies for text contrastive learning. (Shen et al. 2020)

description: Diagram illustrating SimCSE's two modes: unsupervised (same sentence fed twice through encoder with different dropout masks to form positive pairs) and supervised (NLI entailment pairs as positives, contradiction pairs as hard negatives).
teaching_value: Concretely shows how dropout alone can serve as a minimal but effective augmentation strategy, and how NLI supervision provides semantically meaningful positive/negative pairs — clarifying the design choices behind SimCSE.
when_to_show: When teaching how contrastive learning is applied to sentence embeddings, particularly to illustrate the elegance of using dropout noise as augmentation versus explicit label-based supervision.
suggested_caption: SimCSE uses dropout masks (unsupervised) or NLI labels (supervised) for contrastive pairs. (Gao et al. 2021)

description: Results table comparing SimCSE (unsupervised and supervised variants) against baselines including BERT, SBERT, BERT-flow, and BERT-whitening across 7 STS benchmark datasets, reporting Spearman correlation scores.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for the effectiveness of SimCSE, allowing students to compare quantitative gains from different augmentation and supervision strategies and understand the state-of-the-art landscape.
when_to_show: After explaining SimCSE's method, to ground the theoretical discussion in concrete performance numbers and motivate why the approach matters.
suggested_caption: SimCSE achieves strong STS benchmark results across 7 datasets. (Gao et al. 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of Sentence-BERT showing siamese network setup: two BERT encoders sharing weights process sentence pairs, pooled representations are combined and passed to either a softmax classification head (for NLI) or a regression head (for STS).
teaching_value: Illustrates the siamese network architecture pattern for learning sentence embeddings, showing how shared-weight encoders and different prediction heads enable supervised contrastive-style training on NLI data.
when_to_show: When introducing supervised sentence embedding methods, to show how BERT is adapted into a siamese architecture and how different training objectives (classification vs. regression) are applied.
suggested_caption: Sentence-BERT siamese architecture with classification and regression heads. (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019)

description: Performance table of Sentence-BERT on the SentEval benchmark, comparing SBERT against baselines (InferSent, USE, BERT CLS, etc.) across multiple NLP tasks including STS, sentiment analysis, and textual entailment.
teaching_value: Demonstrates SBERT's empirical superiority over prior methods on diverse NLP tasks, helping students understand the practical impact of siamese fine-tuning and the breadth of SentEval as an evaluation suite.
when_to_show: After explaining SBERT's architecture, to show its real-world performance gains and introduce SentEval as a standard evaluation framework for sentence representations.
suggested_caption: SBERT outperforms prior baselines on 5 of 7 SentEval tasks. (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019)

description: Illustration showing BERT-flow's approach: a normalizing flow model maps the anisotropic, non-uniform BERT sentence embedding space to a smooth, isotropic Gaussian distribution, visualized as a transformation from a clustered/skewed space to a uniform spherical distribution.
teaching_value: Visually explains the concept of anisotropy in BERT embeddings and how flow-based calibration corrects it, helping students understand why raw BERT embeddings underperform on similarity tasks and how post-hoc geometric correction helps.
when_to_show: When discussing the geometric properties of pre-trained language model embeddings and methods to improve them without task-specific fine-tuning.
suggested_caption: BERT-flow maps anisotropic BERT embeddings to an isotropic Gaussian space. (Li et al. 2020)

description: Pseudo-code block for the Whitening-k operation, showing steps to compute mean and covariance of sentence vectors, perform SVD decomposition of the covariance matrix, compute transformation matrix W, apply whitening transformation, and optionally reduce to k dimensions.
teaching_value: Provides an algorithmic, step-by-step specification of the whitening transformation, enabling students to understand exactly how sentence embeddings are normalized and dimensionality-reduced using SVD.
when_to_show: When teaching the whitening operation as an alternative to BERT-flow for correcting anisotropic embeddings, especially for students who need to implement or understand the mathematical procedure.
suggested_caption: Pseudo-code for Whitening-k: SVD-based sentence embedding normalization. (Su et al. 2021)

description: Diagram illustrating Quick-Thought vector learning: given a sentence, the model uses two encoders f and g to encode candidate sentences, and learns to classify which candidate is the true context sentence versus negative samples, without a softmax output layer.
teaching_value: Shows how sentence representation learning can be framed as a classification/contrastive problem using context prediction, illustrating the connection between Quick-Thought and NCE/contrastive loss formulations.
when_to_show: When introducing context-based contrastive objectives for sentence embeddings, to show how predicting surrounding sentences provides a self-supervised training signal.
suggested_caption: Quick-Thought learns sentence embeddings by predicting context sentences contrastively. (Logeswaran & Lee, 2018)

description: Architecture diagram of IS-BERT (Info-Sentence BERT) showing how a global sentence representation from BERT's [CLS] token is contrasted with local token-level representations using mutual information maximization via a discriminator network.
teaching_value: Illustrates the Deep InfoMax-inspired approach applied to NLP, showing how maximizing mutual information between local and global representations provides a self-supervised training signal for sentence embeddings.
when_to_show: When teaching mutual information-based contrastive objectives, to show how the local-global MI maximization principle from vision (DIM) transfers to sentence-level NLP tasks.
suggested_caption: IS-BERT maximizes mutual information between local token and global sentence representations. (Zhang et al. 2020)

description: Performance comparison table of IS-BERT on the SentEval benchmark against baselines including InferSent, USE, SBERT, and others, showing scores across supervised and unsupervised settings on multiple NLP tasks.
teaching_value: Provides quantitative evidence for IS-BERT's performance relative to supervised and unsupervised baselines, helping students assess the practical value of mutual information maximization for sentence embeddings.
when_to_show: After explaining IS-BERT's mutual information objective, to contextualize its performance gains and compare unsupervised MI-based methods against supervised approaches like SBERT.
suggested_caption: IS-BERT SentEval results comparing MI-based training to supervised baselines. (Zhang et al. 2020)
